data.title: Deutsche Bank says gold is setting up for a run toward $5,000 in 2026
data.description:
As the year is nearing its end, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has enjoyed a great run, as the metal has surged 58.5% since the start of 2025. 

Gold's golden run might also continue next year. Deutsche Bank has lifted its 2026 forecast to $4,450 an ounce from $4,000/oz and flagged the possibility of prices nearing $5,000 as structural forces tighten the market.

"Stabilizing investor flow and technical measures indicate a positioning correction has completed. Third-quarter supply-demand data supports a continued central bank bid. The positive structural picture shows inelastic demand from central banks and ETF investment diverting supply from the jewelry market. Also, overall growth in demand outpaces supply," Deutsche Bank's Michael Hsueh said. 

Hsueh noted that the metal is breaking historical norms, as its 2025 trading range is the widest since 1980, and its performance as the dollar matches last year's record outperformance. 

"Gold often exhibits a positive correlation to risk, so a deeper equity market correction would be damaging, as would our House view for less Federal Reserve easing than the market expects in 2026 (-50 bps vs -93 bps). A negotiated end to the Russia-Ukraine conflict would be a temporary negative," Hsueh said. 

Hsueh also believes that jewelry demand will remain under pressure in 2026; however, a strict reading of its relationship with year-on-year gold price changes would suggest some upside.

Mine supply, by contrast, remains slow to respond. Even this year’s record quarterly output was overshadowed by stronger official and investor demand, while disruptions at Indonesia’s Grasberg mine loom large over next year’s production outlook. Recycled supply is also recovering only modestly from past highs, constrained by benign macro conditions and consumers’ expectations of further price gains.

Spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) was up 0.7% at $4,159.9. 

Here are some Gold and Gold Miner ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (IAU), (SGOL), (OUNZ), (BAR), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (NUGT), (RING), and (DUST).

data.title: Campbell's backs its soups after exec's alleged rant about ingredients
data.description:
Campbell's (CPB) has defended the ingredients in its soups and placed one of its executives on leave after a lawsuit highlighted his alleged derogatory comments in an audio recording.

The employment discrimination lawsuit was filed by former cybersecurity analyst Robert Garza, who'd been terminated by Campbell's in January.

According to the lawsuit, Garza had a meeting with Martin Bally, Campbell's (CPB) vice president of information technology, last November to discuss his salary.

Garza recorded their conversation, in which Bally allegedly described Campbell's as "highly process[ed] food" for "poor people," and made several racist comments about the company's Indian workers.

"I don't buy Campbell's products barely anymore. It's not healthy now that I know what the f—'s in it," Bally claimed. He also allegedly referenced "bioengineered" meat, saying, "I don't wanna eat a piece of chicken that came from a 3-D printer."

Campbell's (CPB) said Bally is temporarily on leave while it conducts an investigation. "If the comments heard on the audio recording were in fact made by Bally, they are unacceptable," it said. "Such language does not reflect our values and the culture of our company."

The firm said the comments on the recording regarding its food products are "patently absurd," and the chicken in its soups comes from long-trusted, USDA approved suppliers.

"All our soups are made with No Antibiotics Ever chicken meat," it added. "Any claims to the contrary are completely false. Keep in mind, the alleged comments heard on the audio were made by a person in IT, who has nothing to do with how we make our food."

data.title: Silver rises as Chinese stockpiles fall, Fed optimism boosts market
data.description:
Spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) rose 1.7% to $52.37 on Wednesday, set for a third session of gains amid reports that Chinese stockpiles sank to the lowest in a decade and Fed rate cut optimism.

Inventories in warehouses linked to the Shanghai Futures Exchange recently hit the lowest level since 2015, while Shanghai Gold Exchange volumes are back to the smallest in more than nine years, according to bourse and brokerage data, Bloomberg News reported.

The drawdowns came after Chinese exports of the precious metal surged to more than 660 tons in October, the highest volume on record. Reflecting the tightness in China, near-term silver prices have topped later-dated contracts in Shanghai, a pattern known as backwardation that signals short-term pressure, the report said.

China is also one of the world’s largest importers and consumers of silver, which is used extensively in electronics, solar panels, and jewelry, as well as for investment products like silver bars and coins.

“The tightness stems from rising exports to London,” said Zijie Wu, an analyst at Jinrui Futures Co., who also cited industrial and fabrication demand. The shortage may ease in about two months, he said.

Silver has experienced a volatile year, climbing 80% to reach multiple highs. The surge followed gains in gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and speculation that the Trump administration might impose tariffs on the lower-priced metal.

data.title: Nutanix revises FY26 revenue outlook to $2.82B–$2.86B while raising free cash flow target to $800M–$840M as deferred revenue recognition rises
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nutanix (NTNX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Rajiv Ramaswami, President, CEO & Director, stated that "in our first quarter, we saw solid demand for our solutions with bookings that were slightly ahead of our expectations and continued progress with our partners." He added that "the solid performance of the business didn't translate into revenue within the quarter as previously expected. Late in the quarter, we saw more business than expected with start dates outside of the quarter. This resulted in some revenue being shifted out of Q1."
  • Ramaswami emphasized, "our view of the fundamentals of our business and bookings growth expectations for the year remain unchanged. Second, these changes do not impact our free cash flow expectations for FY '26, which we are modestly increasing. And finally, this is solely a timing issue and the amount of revenue we expect to recognize over time from business booked in FY '26 remains unchanged."
  • Rukmini Sivaraman, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO, reported, "in Q1, we reported quarterly revenue of $671 million, within the guided range of $670 million to $680 million, representing a year-over-year growth rate of 13%."
  • Sivaraman noted the shift in revenue timing: "while bookings in Q1 were slightly ahead of our expectations, we saw a larger-than-expected proportion of land and expand bookings with future start dates late in the quarter, resulting in a shift of some revenue from Q1 into future periods."
  • Sivaraman also highlighted, "ARR at the end of Q1 was $2.284 billion, representing year-over-year growth of 18%. NRR or net dollar-based retention rate at the end of Q1 was 109%, flat quarter-over-quarter."

Outlook

  • Sivaraman provided Q2 2026 guidance of revenue between $705 million and $715 million, non-GAAP operating margin of 20.5% to 21.5%, and approximately 296 million fully diluted weighted average shares outstanding.
  • Updated full-year fiscal 2026 guidance is for revenue of $2.82 billion to $2.86 billion, representing a year-over-year growth rate of 12% at the midpoint, and non-GAAP operating margin of 21% to 22%. Free cash flow guidance is raised to $800 million to $840 million, with a free cash flow margin of 28.9% at the midpoint.
  • Sivaraman explained, "it is important to note that our full year bookings growth expectations remain unchanged relative to our last earnings call. We are also pleased to raise our free cash flow guidance for the full year. However, as we saw late in Q1, we are seeing that the timing of conversion of bookings into revenue is evolving with our business."

Financial Results

  • Nutanix reported quarterly revenue of $671 million for Q1 2026, within the guided range, and a year-over-year growth of 13%.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin in Q1 was 88%, and non-GAAP operating margin was 19.7%.
  • Non-GAAP net income in Q1 was $121 million, or $0.41 per share. GAAP net income and fully diluted GAAP EPS were $62 million and $0.21 per share, respectively.
  • Free cash flow in Q1 was $175 million, reflecting a margin of 26%.
  • Cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments stood at $2.062 billion at the end of Q1, up from $1.993 billion at the end of Q4.
  • Nutanix repurchased $50 million of common stock and used $89 million to retire shares related to employees' RSU vesting.

Q&A

  • Simran Biswal, RBC Capital Markets, asked about NRR dynamics and new logos versus expansions. Sivaraman clarified that "new logos in general don't affect NRR," and noted, "overall, we saw the NRR for Q1 as reported stabilized. It was flat quarter-over-quarter."
  • Biswal also inquired about U.S. federal business and government shutdown impacts. Sivaraman responded, "our U.S. Fed business saw double-digit year-over-year growth off of admittedly of a relatively easy comparison last Q1, but we saw nice growth there."
  • James Fish, Piper Sandler, questioned bookings growth and seasonality. Sivaraman confirmed a "26% year-over-year growth in RPO in Q1," and explained that the revenue mix for FY26 is "just a touch more weighted towards second half" compared to FY25.
  • Fish probed on revenue pushouts and supply chain risks. Sivaraman said, "we don't believe supply chain shortages or longer lead times were a meaningful driver of the revenue performance in Q1. However...we believe supply chain tightness could impact the business going forward."
  • Matthew Martino, Goldman Sachs, asked about the evidence for timing versus demand softness. Sivaraman emphasized, "bookings in Q1 were ahead of what we had expected going in."
  • Other analysts focused on migration-related deal timing, OEM partnership impacts, and competitive dynamics, with management consistently affirming that demand remains healthy while revenue recognition is increasingly deferred due to customer preferences and OEM partner shipment timing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a slightly skeptical tone, pressing for clarity on revenue recognition, deal timing, and the potential for demand softness, as well as supply chain impacts. Questions frequently circled back to the sustainability and structural nature of revenue timing shifts.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly defensive tone, frequently reiterating, "we believe the underlying value of our business remains unchanged." Ramaswami and Sivaraman consistently described the revenue guidance change as a timing issue rather than demand weakness, and used phrases like "we believe" and "we are monitoring this closely."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone was more focused on deferred revenue and structural business changes, while management shifted from confidence in growth to more detailed explanations of revenue timing and mitigation strategies for supply chain risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most notable shift from Q4 2025 is the reduction in full-year fiscal 2026 revenue guidance from $2.9 billion–$2.94 billion to $2.82 billion–$2.86 billion, attributed to deferred revenue recognition rather than lower bookings or demand.
  • Free cash flow guidance increased from $790 million–$830 million to $800 million–$840 million, reflecting continued strong cash collection despite revenue timing changes.
  • Management tone shifted from celebrating above-guidance performance to explaining new business mix dynamics and providing reassurances regarding underlying demand.
  • Analysts in the current quarter concentrated on deal timing, OEM partner revenue recognition, and the long-term structural implications of these changes, compared to broader demand and margin questions in the previous quarter.
  • Management emphasized the structural nature of OEM partner revenue recognition and customer preferences for flexible license start dates, both of which did not feature as prominently in the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified a risk of supply chain tightness due to industry-wide component shortages, which could impact hardware shipments by OEM partners and further delay revenue recognition.
  • The evolving timing of bookings conversion into revenue, driven by customer preferences and OEM partner dynamics, introduces variability and potential forecasting challenges.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of current demand trends, the structural nature of timing shifts, and potential for delayed revenue to impact future growth visibility.
  • Management outlined mitigation strategies, including expanding server partner options, increasing hardware compatibility, supporting external storage, and broadening public cloud offerings.

Final Takeaway

Nutanix management emphasized that while the timing of revenue recognition has shifted due to more bookings with future start dates and a growing portion of business through OEM partners, the company’s underlying demand, bookings growth, and free cash flow trajectory remain robust. The reduction in fiscal 2026 revenue guidance reflects this timing shift, not a deterioration in business fundamentals, and is offset by an increase in free cash flow guidance. Management remains focused on maintaining strong customer relationships and expanding platform capabilities while working to mitigate supply chain and timing-related risks.
